1â†’If {rbrn_uplink_dns_mode} is disabled, the {at_sentry_container}:
     2â†’
     3â†’* Blocks all DNS traffic in RBM-FORWARD from eth1
     4â†’* Blocks all DNS traffic in RBM-EGRESS to eth0
     5â†’* Removes any existing DNS-related NAT rules
     6â†’
     7â†’If {rbrn_uplink_dns_mode} is global or allowlist, the {at_sentry_container} executes this sequence:
     8â†’
     9â†’. DNS Server Validation:
    10â†’+
    11â†’--
    12â†’* Verify {rbrr_dns_server} if specified:
    13â†’** TCP connect test to port 53 with 5 second timeout
    14â†’** UDP query for "." record with 6 second timeout
    15â†’* Fail if either test unsuccessful
    16â†’--
    17â†’
    18â†’. Dnsmasq Configuration:
    19â†’+
    20â†’--
    21â†’* Core Settings:
    22â†’** Interface binding limited strictly to {at_enclave_network} interface eth1
    23â†’** Use {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip} for listen-address
    24â†’** DNS service only - all DHCP functionality explicitly disabled
    25â†’** DNS resolution configuration isolated from host system settings
    26â†’** Clear existing upstream server configuration
    27â†’** Configure upstream DNS:
    28â†’*** Use configured upstream server when {rbrr_dns_server} specified
    29â†’*** Otherwise use host resolver via external network interface
    30â†’
    31â†’* Cache Settings:
    32â†’** Cache size: 1000 entries
    33â†’** Minimum TTL: 600 seconds
    34â†’** Maximum TTL: 3600 seconds
    35â†’
    36â†’* Query Control:
    37â†’** When {rbrn_uplink_dns_mode} is global:
    38â†’*** Accept all DNS queries from eth1
    39â†’*** Forward queries unmodified to upstream
    40â†’*** Log each query with timestamp and resolution
    41â†’*** Return all responses unmodified
    42â†’
    43â†’** When {rbrn_uplink_dns_mode} is allowlist:
    44â†’*** Load allowed domains from {rbrn_uplink_allowed_domains}
    45â†’*** Accept queries only for allowed domains
    46â†’*** Return NXDOMAIN for all other queries
    47â†’*** Configure dnsmasq logging directly with:
    48â†’**** log-queries=extra
    49â†’**** log-facility=/var/log/dnsmasq.log
    50â†’**** log-dhcp
    51â†’**** Extra logging options in dnsmasq.conf:
    52â†’***** log-debug (for detailed query info)
    53â†’***** log-async=20 (for better performance)
    54â†’--
    55â†’
    56â†’. Filter Rules:
    57â†’+
    58â†’--
    59â†’* Accept DNS traffic in RBM-FORWARD from eth1:
    60â†’** UDP port 53
    61â†’** TCP port 53
    62â†’* Accept DNS forwarding in RBM-EGRESS to eth0:
    63â†’** UDP/TCP port 53 to {rbrr_dns_server}
    64â†’** Or to host resolver if no server specified
    65â†’--
    66â†’
    67â†’. NAT Configuration:
    68â†’+
    69â†’--
    70â†’* DNAT eth1 DNS to {rbrn_enclave_sentry_ip}:53
    71â†’* SNAT outbound queries to eth0 IP
    72â†’* Preserve existing port forwarding rules
    73â†’--
    74â†’

1â†’= Getting Started with Recipe Bottle
     2â†’
     3â†’{at_recipe_bottle}
     4â†’constructs container images under controlled conditions on trusted
     5â†’{gcp_service}
     6â†’infrastructure.
     7â†’Developers retrieve these
     8â†’{at_bottle_image_s}
     9â†’and work within identical environments.
    10â†’The
    11â†’{rbtgi_repository}
    12â†’serves as the single source of truth for container artifacts.
    13â†’
    14â†’This guide explains the setup sequence and ongoing operational patterns.
    15â†’Most of what follows happens once; the final sections describe day-to-day work.
    16â†’
    17â†’== Core Concepts
    18â†’
    19â†’{at_recipe_bottle} contains workloads safely.
    20â†’A _vessel_ defines what to build--a specification for a container workload.
    21â†’An {rbtga_ark} is the built result: the immutable artifact produced from that vessel.
    22â†’Multiple {rbtga_ark_s} can exist for a single vessel, each with its own build timestamp and provenance.
    23â†’
    24â†’Each {rbtga_ark} carries strong pedigree information including SBOMs and build metadata.
    25â†’Organizations verify these attestations before deploying.
    26â†’
    27â†’== Depots and Roles
    28â†’
    29â†’A
    30â†’{rbtgi_depot}
    31â†’is the logical facility where container images are constructed and retained.
    32â†’The
    33â†’{rbtgi_repository}
    34â†’within the {rbtgi_depot} stores all
    35â†’{rbtgi_image_s}
    36â†’available for retrieval.
    37â†’Most organizations require exactly one {rbtgi_depot}.
    38â†’
    39â†’{at_recipe_bottle}
    40â†’provides bash command-line administration of {rbtgi_depot} lifecycle and operations across roles.
    41â†’
    42â†’* *{rbtr_payor}*: Creates and funds {rbtgi_depot_s}; creates {rbtr_governor_s}. Billing flows through the {rbtr_payor_p} linked account. Manual OAuth setup enables all subsequent automation.
    43â†’* *{rbtr_governor}*: Administers credentials within a {rbtgi_depot}. Creates {rbtr_director_s} and {rbtr_retriever_s} via service account authentication.
    44â†’* *{rbtr_director}*: Submits builds, manages {rbtgi_image_s}.
    45â†’* *{rbtr_retriever}*: Pulls {rbtgi_image_s} for local use.
    46â†’
    47â†’The
    48â†’{rbtr_payor}
    49â†’stands apart--requires manual console work and OAuth authentication for both {rbtgi_depot} and {rbtr_governor} lifecycle operations.
    50â†’The
    51â†’{rbtr_governor}
    52â†’and all downstream roles authenticate via
    53â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}
    54â†’credentials, enabling automation without human interaction.
    55â†’
    56â†’== Phase 1: Establish
    57â†’
    58â†’{rbtgo_payor_establish}
    59â†’is a guide for manual steps taken using the Google Cloud Console to create the
    60â†’{rbtgi_payor_project},
    61â†’link a billing account,
    62â†’enable required APIs,
    63â†’and configure an OAuth 2.0 client.
    64â†’
    65â†’This phase produces a
    66â†’{rbtgi_json_key_file}
    67â†’containing OAuth client credentials.
    68â†’This file must not be committed to version control.
    69â†’
    70â†’== Phase 2: Setup
    71â†’
    72â†’The administrator who completed establishment now executes automation from their
    73â†’{at_workstation}.
    74â†’
    75â†’{rbtgo_payor_install}
    76â†’takes the
    77â†’{rbtgi_json_key_file}
    78â†’and completes the OAuth handshake.
    79â†’A browser window opens for authorization.
    80â†’Upon success, the
    81â†’{rbro_refresh_token}
    82â†’is stored in `~/.rbw/rbro.env` with `600` permissions.
    83â†’This file enables the
    84â†’{rbtr_payor}
    85â†’to authenticate for all future operations without repeating the browser flow.
    86â†’
    87â†’{rbtgo_depot_create}
    88â†’provisions the container build infrastructure.
    89â†’Most installations require exactly one
    90â†’{rbtgi_depot}-
    91â†’a cohesive unit comprising a dedicated
    92â†’{gcp_project_id},
    93â†’a
    94â†’{rbtgi_build_bucket},
    95â†’a
    96â†’{rbtgi_repository},
    97â†’and the
    98â†’{rbtr_mason}
    99â†’service account that executes builds within the cloud.
   100â†’
   101â†’The
   102â†’{rbtr_payor}
   103â†’can manage multiple {rbtgi_depot_s}:
   104â†’{rbtgo_depot_list}
   105â†’displays all active {rbtgi_depot_s} and their status;
   106â†’{rbtgo_depot_destroy}
   107â†’permanently removes a {rbtgi_depot} and all its resources.
   108â†’
   109â†’The
   110â†’{rbtr_payor}
   111â†’then executes
   112â†’{rbtgo_governor_reset}
   113â†’to create the
   114â†’{rbtr_governor}
   115â†’service account within the
   116â†’{rbtgi_depot}.
   117â†’This {rbtr_payor} operation produces an
   118â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file},
   119â†’stored at the path specified by
   120â†’{rbrr_governor_rbra_file}.
   121â†’The
   122â†’{rbtr_governor}
   123â†’can now operate independently of the
   124â†’{rbtr_payor}.
   125â†’
   126â†’== Credential Administration
   127â†’
   128â†’The
   129â†’{rbtr_governor}
   130â†’manages the roster of credentialed users throughout the
   131â†’{rbtgi_depot}
   132â†’lifecycle.
   133â†’While credential creation is typically infrequent, this administrative function remains active.
   134â†’
   135â†’{rbtgo_director_create}
   136â†’provisions a
   137â†’{rbtr_director}
   138â†’service account, producing an
   139â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}
   140â†’at the path specified by
   141â†’{rbrr_director_rbra_file}.
   142â†’Directors submit builds and manage images.
   143â†’
   144â†’{rbtgo_retriever_create}
   145â†’provisions a
   146â†’{rbtr_retriever}
   147â†’service account, producing an
   148â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}
   149â†’at the path specified by
   150â†’{rbrr_retriever_rbra_file}.
   151â†’Retrievers pull images for local use.
   152â†’
   153â†’The
   154â†’{rbtr_governor}
   155â†’also maintains visibility into issued credentials through
   156â†’{rbtgo_sa_list}
   157â†’and can revoke access through
   158â†’{rbtgo_sa_delete}.
   159â†’
   160â†’== Credential Files
   161â†’
   162â†’Each {rbtr_role} maintains its own credential file.
   163â†’Understanding these files helps administrators plan secure handling.
   164â†’
   165â†’The
   166â†’{rbtr_payor}
   167â†’credentials reside in `~/.rbw/rbro.env`, containing the
   168â†’{rbro_client_secret}
   169â†’and
   170â†’{rbro_refresh_token}.
   171â†’This file exists only on the administrator's
   172â†’{at_workstation}.
   173â†’
   174â†’The
   175â†’{rbtr_governor},
   176â†’{rbtr_director},
   177â†’and
   178â†’{rbtr_retriever}
   179â†’each have an
   180â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}
   181â†’at paths defined in the repository configuration.
   182â†’Each file contains
   183â†’{giam_service_account}
   184â†’credentials scoped to one role within one
   185â†’{rbtgi_depot}.
   186â†’
   187â†’All credential files require `600` permissions, exclusion from version control, and secure distribution to authorized users.
   188â†’A credential file cannot operate outside its designated role and {rbtgi_depot}.
   189â†’
   190â†’== Day-to-Day Operations
   191â†’
   192â†’Once setup and credential administration are complete, ongoing work proceeds through
   193â†’{rbtr_director}
   194â†’and
   195â†’{rbtr_retriever}
   196â†’roles.
   197â†’
   198â†’=== Director Operations
   199â†’
   200â†’The
   201â†’{rbtr_director}
   202â†’manages the build lifecycle.
   203â†’
   204â†’{rbtgo_trigger_build}
   205â†’submits a container build to
   206â†’{gcb_service}.
   207â†’The
   208â†’{rbtr_mason}
   209â†’executes the build within the cloud and publishes the resulting
   210â†’{rbtgi_image}
   211â†’to the
   212â†’{rbtgi_repository}.
   213â†’
   214â†’{rbtgo_image_delete}
   215â†’removes an
   216â†’{rbtgi_image}
   217â†’from the
   218â†’{rbtgi_repository}.
   219â†’
   220â†’**MISSING**: {rbtgi_image} listing operation is not yet implemented.
   221â†’
   222â†’=== Retriever Operations
   223â†’
   224â†’The
   225â†’{rbtr_retriever}
   226â†’accesses built artifacts.
   227â†’
   228â†’{rbtgo_image_retrieve}
   229â†’pulls an
   230â†’{rbtgi_image}
   231â†’from the
   232â†’{rbtgi_repository}
   233â†’to the local
   234â†’{at_workstation}.
   235â†’
   236â†’== Recovery
   237â†’
   238â†’If `~/.rbw/rbro.env` is lost, the administrator obtains a fresh
   239â†’{rbtgi_json_key_file}
   240â†’from Google Cloud Console and re-executes
   241â†’{rbtgo_payor_install}.
   242â†’
   243â†’If OAuth tokens expire,
   244â†’{rbtgo_payor_refresh}
   245â†’obtains fresh credentials.
   246â†’
   247â†’If
   248â†’{rbtr_governor}
   249â†’credentials are compromised or need rotation, the
   250â†’{rbtr_payor}
   251â†’executes
   252â†’{rbtgo_governor_reset}
   253â†’to replace the existing
   254â†’{rbtr_governor}
   255â†’service account with a fresh one, generating new
   256â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}
   257â†’credentials.
   258â†’
   259â†’To revoke a user's access, the
   260â†’{rbtr_governor}
   261â†’executes
   262â†’{rbtgo_sa_delete}
   263â†’for the target service account, invalidating the corresponding
   264â†’{rbtgi_rbra_file}.
   265â†’

    26â†’|`docker-container`
    27â†’|Supported
    28â†’|Isolated container, no host credentials
    29â†’|===
    30â†’
    31â†’The `docker-container` driver creates an isolated BuildKit container that does not inherit host Docker daemon credentials.
    32â†’This isolation is architecturalâ€”credentials configured via `docker login` in one
    33â†’{gcb_build}
    34â†’step are not available to the buildx builder container in subsequent steps.
    35â†’
    36â†’Additional constraints discovered:
    37â†’
    38â†’* **DOCKER_CONFIG environment variable**: Known bug (https://github.com/docker/cli/issues/5477[docker/cli #5477]) breaks buildx command parsing
    39â†’* **Kaniko alternative**: Project archived January 2025, no longer maintained
    40â†’* **GCR vs GAR identical**: Testing confirmed 403 Forbidden occurs with both `gcr.io` and
    41â†’{gar_registry}â€”problem is universal to docker-container driver isolation
    42â†’* **buildkitd.toml registry auth**: No OAuth token support documented; primarily certificate-based examples
    43â†’
    44â†’==== Alternatives Evaluated
    45â†’
    46â†’[cols="1,3,1",options="header"]
    47â†’|===
    48â†’|Option
    49â†’|Approach
    50â†’|Disposition
    51â†’
    52â†’|DOCKER_CONFIG
    53â†’|Pass config.json location via environment variable
    54â†’|Eliminated: cli bug #5477
    55â†’
    56â†’|buildkitd.toml
    57â†’|Configure registry auth in BuildKit config file
    58â†’|Eliminated: no
    59â†’{oauth_token}
    60â†’support
    61â†’
    62â†’|Kaniko
    63â†’|Switch to Google's containerized build tool
    64â†’|Eliminated: project archived
    65â†’
    66â†’|Single-architecture
    67â†’|Build only linux/amd64 using default driver
    68â†’|Rejected: defeats multi-platform goal
    69â†’
    70â†’|Manual manifest combining
    71â†’|Build each arch separately, combine manifests
    72â†’|Rejected: excessive complexity
    73â†’|===
    74â†’
    75â†’==== Chosen Solution
    76â†’
    77â†’**OCI Layout Bridge**: Separate the build phase from the push phase using OCI Image Layout as intermediate format.
    78â†’
    79â†’The key insight: instead of fighting BuildKit's isolation to make `--push` work, break the atomic operation into discrete phases with filesystem storage as bridge.
    80â†’
    81â†’----
    82â†’Build Phase (no credentials needed):
    83â†’  docker buildx build --output type=oci,dest=/workspace/oci-layout.tar ...
    84â†’
    85â†’Push Phase (has credentials via metadata server):
    86â†’  skopeo copy --all --dest-registry-token "${TOKEN}" \
    87â†’    "oci-archive:/workspace/oci-layout.tar" \
    88â†’    "docker://${IMAGE_URI}"
    89â†’----
    90â†’
    91â†’==== Rationale
    92â†’
    93â†’* **Separation of concerns**: BuildKit builds (what it does best), Skopeo pushes (what it does best)
    94â†’* **No credential propagation needed**: Build phase writes to local filesystem, push phase uses
    95â†’{gcb_service}
    96â†’metadata server
    97â†’* **Google-endorsed**: Skopeo explicitly recommended in https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/developers-practitioners/five-ways-skopeo-can-simplify-your-google-cloud-container-workflow[Google Cloud blog]
    98â†’* **Token timeout eliminated**: Build phase can take hours; push phase takes seconds with fresh
    99â†’{oauth_token}
   100â†’* **{gcb_service} native**: `/workspace` shared across steps provides natural bridge
   101â†’
   102â†’==== Implementation Details
   103â†’
   104â†’**Build step** (rbgjb06-build-and-export.sh):
   105â†’----
   106â†’docker buildx create --driver docker-container --name rb-builder --use
   107â†’docker buildx build \
   108â†’  --platform="${_RBGY_PLATFORMS}" \
   109â†’  --output type=oci,dest=/workspace/oci-layout.tar \
   110â†’  --tag ${IMAGE}:${TAG} \
   111â†’  ...
   112â†’----
   113â†’
   114â†’**Push step** (rbgjb07-push-with-skopeo.sh):
   115â†’----
   116â†’AR_TOKEN=$(curl -s -H "Metadata-Flavor: Google" \
   117â†’  "http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token" | \
   118â†’  sed -n 's/.*"access_token":"\([^"]*\)".*/\1/p')
   119â†’
   120â†’skopeo copy --all \
   121â†’  --dest-registry-token "${AR_TOKEN}" \
   122â†’  "oci-archive:/workspace/oci-layout.tar" \
   123â†’  "docker://${IMAGE_URI}"
   124â†’----
   125â†’
   126â†’**Critical implementation notes**:
   127â†’
   128â†’* Use `--dest-registry-token` not `--dest-creds` for OAuth bearer
   129â†’{oauth_token_s}
   130â†’* Use tarball format (default), not `tar=false` directory format (has annotation bugs per https://github.com/moby/buildkit/issues/5572[moby/buildkit #5572])
   131â†’* Skopeo's `oci-archive:` transport reads tarballs directly
   132â†’* Syft cannot analyze multi-platform OCI archives directly; must pull image first and analyze via docker socket
   133â†’* {rbtr_mason}
   134â†’requires `roles/artifactregistry.admin` on the
   135â†’{rbtgi_repository}
   136â†’resource (not project-level) for Skopeo push to succeed
   137â†’
   138â†’==== References
   139â†’
   140â†’* Research memo: link:RBWMBX-BuildxMultiPlatformAuth.adoc[RBWMBX: Buildx Multi-Platform Authentication Memo]
   141â†’* Verified working: Cloud Build 3b544930 (2025-12-31)
   142â†’
